flashcards.q: Define electric current (I).
flashcards.a: The amount of charge (Q) flowing through a particular area in unit time (t); I = Q/t.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of electric charge?
What is the SI unit of electric current?
What constitutes the flow of charges in metallic wires?
What is the charge on a single electron?
Define an electric circuit.
flashcards.a: A continuous and closed path of an electric current.

flashcards.q: What is the function of a switch in a circuit?
flashcards.a: To make or break the conducting link between the cell and the bulb.

flashcards.q: Name the instrument used to measure electric current and its connection mode.
Define electric potential difference (V).
flashcards.a: The work done (W) to move a unit charge (Q) from one point to another; V = W/Q.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of potential difference?
Name the instrument used to measure potential difference and its connection mode.
State Ohm's Law.
flashcards.a: V ∝ I (at constant temperature); V = IR.

flashcards.q: Define 'Resistance' of a conductor.
flashcards.a: The property of a conductor to resist the flow of charges through it.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of resistance?
What is 'Variable Resistance' or a 'Rheostat'?
flashcards.a: A component used to regulate current without changing the voltage source.

flashcards.q: On what factors does the resistance of a uniform metallic conductor depend?
flashcards.a: Length (l), Area of cross-section (A), and nature of material.

flashcards.q: Define 'Electrical Resistivity' (ρ).
flashcards.a: A characteristic property of a material measuring its opposition to current flow; R = ρ(l/A).

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of resistivity?
Which type of materials have very low resistivity (10^-8 to 10^-6 Ωm)?
What is the equivalent resistance (Rs) of resistors joined in series?
What is the equivalent resistance (Rp) of resistors joined in parallel?
In which combination (series or parallel) is the current same through all resistors?
In which combination (series or parallel) is the potential difference same across all resistors?
State Joule's Law of heating.
flashcards.a: H = I^2 Rt; heat produced is proportional to square of current, resistance, and time.

flashcards.q: What is the primary function of an electric fuse?
flashcards.a: A safety device that breaks the circuit during overloading or short circuits by melting.

flashcards.q: Define electric power (P).
flashcards.a: The rate of consumption or dissipation of electric energy; P = VI.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of electric power?
What is the commercial unit of electric energy?
How many Joules are in 1 kWh?
Why is tungsten used for bulb filaments?
flashcards.a: It has a very high melting point (3380 °C) and does not melt at light-emitting temperatures.

flashcards.q: Why are copper and aluminum wires used for transmission lines?
flashcards.a: They have very low resistivity, minimizing energy loss as heat.
